02:37**\
Ray Batra\
That I wanted to go through. So the first thing is I want to make sure.
We want to make sure that we understand if we\'re boiling down the
essence of the value prop of career span for the job. Seeker. There\'s
at least two things, maybe three things if I understand it correctly.
Sort of. One is that there is an element that careerspan is a bit like a
common app for them so that you can fill it out once and then it can be
considered for many. Right, Yep. And then a second thing is that
there\'s the promise, although we\'ll talk about that in a second. But
the promise of basically better matches via stories, like filling out
your stories is sort of a secondary thing. And then I guess to a third
extent there\'s the element of coaching. Like that happens along the
way.

**\
03:21**\
Ray Batra\
Right. Is that accurate?

**\
03:26**\
Logan Currie\
Yes. And the thing that I would add as well, which is kind of like, I

how they come in. But it would also be the idea of like skipping the
queue to it to a certain extent by not by getting a second look from a
hiring manager because somebody is coming and if you\'re qualified and
you\'re one of the like the top candidates, you know that you\'re going
to get a look essentially beyond the ATS lottery.

**\
03:56**\
Ray Batra\
Okay. So, yeah, so I was sort of, I\'m going to throw that in the

okay.

Yeah, that\'s largely correct. Yeah.

04:08**\

Okay. The second question I have is. And don\'t. This is not intended to

**\
04:36**\
Logan Currie\
Perfect timing because were literally just on a call digging into this.
So I would probably divide that better matching into two pieces. One is
around the employer and what they care about seeing because that is
essentially it\'s very employer dependent. Right. Like if they\'re like,
okay, cool, yeah, I want to see fohee members. I\'m going to consider 10

at which I\'m going to consider them pre filter if I\'m working with a

employers that we are working directly with to source roles. So it\'s
not zero, but it\'s. I can talk you through what that process looks like
on the better matching side of things.

**\
05:21**\
Logan Currie\
I was just thinking V that it would be a good case study the like
example that were talking about earlier. But we have a.

**\

We\'re putting from Fohy or not Trevor Justin was from Fohy. Nevermind.

Logan Currie\
Anyway, we have quite a few case studies that I can send through to you
of a person based on their resume who would not have been put forward

they become a much stronger candidate. Like literally both from ATS
blind test perspective from a. Oh wow. This person if they just position
themselves in this way because we know this additional information
they\'re now at the top of this kind of proverbial stack because
they\'ve. They\'ve told the career span stories. Yeah, actually those
that I can.

**\
06:13**\
Vrijen Attawar\
We actually even have. We even have yesterday I was looking at with ilsa
we even have statistical evidence to sort of indicate how much more of
an individual you can see with the context of career span stories. And I
think this is especially relevant for. For, for this crowd and for FOHI
crowd because you know, I think anyone that\'s had qualitative strengths
and tried to communicate those resumes will realize it is a very hard
thing to do. Right. They don\'t necessarily capture that dimension of
who someone is and it generally puts folks that are non data, non
engineering, those sort of folks who have these hard skill labels that
they can drop on their resume just inarguably from a structural
perspective it puts them at a disadvantage.

**\
06:57**\
Vrijen Attawar\
What we have is the ability to show how much more of someone\'s soft
skills we have learned about them after. After a few conversations.

**\
07:06**\
Ray Batra\
But to just make sure I\'m like clear on that. So the. There are, there
are a few employers like four or something on the employer side. But
then in terms of evidence on the side of the claim that this will lead
to better matches via both the story piece and skipping the cue piece,
there are at least one if not a couple kind of case studies where
someone that wouldn\'t have been put forward with just a resume but with
the additional stories they\'re done. There\'s a case study piece. I

it.

**\
07:51**\
Logan Currie\
Number one, this is also valuable as a valuable line of questioning
because it echoes essentially what we\'re putting together on our side
too. Right. So in terms of the metrics, there\'s. We were just chatting
about this actually, but we\'re dividing the kind of pipeline into three
where it\'s like candidates put forward for pre screening and like kind
of co selected by the employer. Right. So pre screening is one thing
where it\'s like actually yeah, I do want to talk to this person again,
that depends on where they\'re sourcing and how their process is already
working in some ways. Then it\'s into the interview process, then it\'s
offer made and accepted. So it\'s like how those are the. Based on where
these candidates that we put forward, this is the short list sourced by
career span put into your pipeline.

**\
08:40**\
Logan Currie\
How many of those people make it to job offer? That\'s the gold
standard, I would say. But additionally, how many of those do you want
to talk to and to continue in this interview process? Yeah, that\'s the
kind of attribution tracking that we\'re doing of how far do they make
it through essentially.

**\
09:02**\
Ray Batra\
Oh, sorry, go ahead.

**\
09:04**\
Logan Currie\
I was going to. The outcome of the stories meant more for your resume or
for an interview. Like what is the primary purpose?

**\
09:12**\
Vrijen Attawar\
The, the primary, the value of stories is twofold. There is the direct
value of just saying the story and having it stored away and that\'s
just for the individual. And then there is the matching value of having
that expanded pool of insight into someone that you can use to better
assess them for certain roles. And so that\'s what I was referring to.
As far as the lift is concerned. If you think of like every skill and
associated piece of evidence for that skill as like individual data
points, you can just numerically get more indicators of a particular
trait or quality using the stories based approach as opposed to just,
you know, a resume which is a pretty superficial piece of information.

**\
09:59**\
Logan Currie\
But to your point Shivani like it does help them create a resume that is
custom to that role. The idea that we\'re moving towards is that you
wouldn\'t need that piece of paper. Yeah, that can work.

**\
10:11**\
Vrijen Attawar\
We do both for you. Exactly. We give you, we help you with the status
quo need of a resume and a tailored resume of that and we also help you
with the sort of work that match you with other.

**\
10:24**\
Logan Currie\
Folks and are the people who are coming on the platform right now. Are
they mostly there to like put their information out there so that
employers can find them? Are they more so there to find those stories,

**\
10:42**\
Vrijen Attawar\
Because there is, up until now it\'s been purely just come in to help

**\
10:52**\
Logan Currie\
No, we literally updated our website yesterday to openly talk about the

last three months, which is essentially we deliver jobs to you. And now
it is reflected on our website essentially that we are now like working
with employers directly as well.

**\
11:10**\
Vrijen Attawar\
What I can, what I can certainly speak to is how much do folks want
careerspan jobs? Or you know, as another sort of indication of how good
the matching system is, how happy are people with the recommendations
that we make? Right. And in that regard we have Generally speaking about
50 ish percent like day 30 returns for users that have been receiving
jobs closer to like 20, 30% day 60. So it\'s, you know, it\'s big
numbers as far as people, you know, again with that like 80, 90% open
rate, like 12 to 15% click through rate like that. People, people really
only see jobs and we maintain a high standard for that. People only see
jobs that they genuinely feel like they have a chance with. And so that
sort of is why we\'re starting to see much higher numbers.

**\
12:01**\
Jake Weissbourd\
Awesome. I got a question just about like supply and demand. So like
let\'s say were going to blast out an email to 5,000 FOHI members saying
if you\'re open to changing jobs or you\'re actively looking to change
jobs, you should learn about our partner career span and you should fill
out a profile and let\'s say you get a flood of like 700 signups the
next. Do you have enough jobs on the platform that could like the supply
of jobs that could meet that new crazy influx of demand for jobs? Or
would we find 700 people who are fighting for like three jobs and not be
so.

**\
12:44**\
Vrijen Attawar\
That\'s a great question. That\'s why we distribute scraped jobs and we
have a pretty high standard for those scraped jobs is because we know
that people want jobs, right? And they deserve to get like good job
recommendations made to them. We make it clear which jobs are being
sourced through careerspan and where they will actually get that lift of
being seen before everyone else or parallel to everyone else and which
jobs are just sort of like delivered by or kind of aggregated by
careerspan. That\'s the functionality that is relatively expensive. So
we offer it for free for quite some time. And that\'s the only component
of career span that we would consider putting a paywall up for. And
obviously we would. If that was with a fohee member, we\'d split that
revenue.

**\
13:29**\
Vrijen Attawar\
But that\'s the only thing that we would really drop a paywall in for.
But for about a month or two folks get those tailored jobs delivered to
them anyway and we just increase the proportion of jobs we ingest with
the size of our, the people receiving jobs.

**\
13:46**\
Ray Batra\
So two things there. Right? So, so what\'s the, if we\'re talking like
for employers, like what\'s the distribution on that? Right? So is it,
are we talking like there\'ll be five jobs, 10 jobs that are sort of
career span kind of, you know, delivered, you know, through with

were to do this together start with like a smaller distribution Jake,
rather than like an entire thing? Because I share Jake\'s concern. I
think it\'s a great question but also I\'m just curious if it just
becomes an aggregator. I don\'t like is that even like worth it? Because
that kind of essentially takes away pretty much all of the value prop.
Right. For like it\'s.

**\
14:37**\
Ray Batra\
It\'s not really a common app. Right. And it\'s also doesn\'t. It also
isn\'t necessarily better matches so.

**\
14:44**\
Logan Currie\
It still acts as a common app in the sense that whatever information we

**\
15:01**\
Ray Batra\
Yeah, yeah, but from a notification perspective. But not from. It

apply to multiple things like for the aggregation. No. So if they\'re

15:14**\
Vrijen Attawar\
It doesn\'t help you apply. It doesn\'t help you apply to multiple

15:25**\
Ray Batra\
Well, actually I want to clarify. So forget the Aggregation piece for a

15:32**\
Logan Currie\
Yes.

Ray Batra\
What is the function of career span in terms of like for the job seeker.
Job seeker sees a job through careerspan that\'s through one of your

**\
15:54**\
Vrijen Attawar\
Yeah.

**\
15:55**\
Ray Batra\
Do you see what I\'m saying?

Vrijen Attawar\
Yeah, so right.

**\

Ray Batra\
I get what you\'re saying, I get what you\'re saying from this, from the

**\
16:09**\
Vrijen Attawar\
Right, so that\'s where we, you know, as Logan said at the beginning, we
have handful of employers that have put maybe 10 jobs or so up there.
That is sort of my job in the sort of dynamic is to go out and sort of
get those job seekers on board, get VCs as channel partners, all that
stuff. So you know to again to contextualize. We\'ve been sort of moving
towards that motion for about a month. But yes, very much a ground zero
on that side. I think where we, where that lead times actually helpful
and where the distributed jobs actually sort of get things going is from
if you think of it from an individual\'s perspective it is, you know and
from the average job seekers perspective it gives them a reason to
engage with careerspan to fill out gaps.

**\
16:53**\
Vrijen Attawar\
It\'s almost this sort of like Runway that you need to engage with the
product to start being matched at a high level. Right. Again, if we use
that example of the person Logan gave, they were 10, maybe 15 points
below the threshold to be recommended. Did a bunch of stories past our
recommendation threshold are now being put forward to employers and so
there is still intrinsic merit in folks working towards those jobs. But
we, our hope is very much to increase the proportion of jobs we get from
employers and to serve as the infrastructure for you to distribute any
jobs that come your way.

**\
17:29**\
Ray Batra\
So I\'m still not clear though. I want to just be crystal clear about
this.

**\
17:31**\
Vrijen Attawar\
Right.

**\
17:32**\
Ray Batra\
So if I\'m a job seeker and if I\'m going to, if I\'m applying, if I\'ve
been shown 10 jobs or five jobs that are through career spans, direct
relationship network with employers, the job seeker is still going to
need to fill out those other five to 10 separate applications or no,
this is.

**\
17:53**\
Logan Currie\
Where it\'s employer dependent. So two of them have Said like, listen,
just introduce them. They\'re in our pipeline. Two of them have said
they still need to apply for the like, they would still need to fill out
the application on our website to be put into our existing ATS system.
So again, it\'s going to be, I think, employer dependent. We have
existing, we have the ability to integrate with ATS systems. So it\'s
not technically hard to essentially take any of this career span
information, integrate with Greenhouse Lever, whatever it is, and
essentially put them into that pipeline. We just need the employer to
agree to like, essentially see that pipeline. So obviously working
towards to be like, okay, cool, I apply and I actually apply.

**\
18:31**\
Vrijen Attawar\
Yeah, they all have open APIs to do that. So it\'s just a matter of
getting sufficiently effective us as a company.

**\
18:37**\
Ray Batra\
Okay, so that answers my question. Depends on the employer. There\'s a
few of them, a couple of them that do that right now. Hopefully more on
the way, but it\'s not a guarantee for all of them. That makes sense.
Jake, anything else out of Shivani on that?

18:48**\
Jake Weissbourd\
Not specifically on that Shivani.

**\
18:50**\
Vrijen Attawar\
Did you have anything there?

**\
18:51**\
Shivani Mathur\
No.

**\
18:52**\
Jake Weissbourd\
I\'m hoping you can share a bit more about the business model and how
you\'re. It sounds like you\'re not charging job seekers yet, but there
may be some paywall feature in the future. Can you help us understand
how you\'re charging employers what you\'re charging employers? Is it
pay per success? Is it pay per lead? How does it work?

**\
19:15**\
Vrijen Attawar\
Sure. So the, I mean, with employers as well and in something as
established as hiring, there\'s obviously a lot of. You\'re competing
for a lot of attention or competing with a lot of other people. The way
we\'re trying to bootstrap up the amount we earn from employers is start
by saying, hey, we will run the scan for you, we\'ll give you
candidates, we anonymize those candidates and we essentially say, hey,
pay us for a warm intro. We\'re starting at the \$300 to \$500 range and
going to level that up. But that to us represents a very easy proof is
in the pudding. If you like the candidate, take them, don\'t like them,
no skin off your back. We try to limit that to like three to five
candidates at a time. So it\'s pretty easy. Started seeing a little bit
of momentum on that.

**\
20:01**\
Vrijen Attawar\
There\'s closer to about seven or eight employers that are open to or
are sending us sort of jds for that, but that\'s where we\'re starting.
What we do hope to eventually do is essentially be both in terms of
signal and in terms of pricing, a alternative to the recruiter that is
more trustworthy than inbound but higher volume and more reliable than
referrals. Right, Cool.

**\
20:28**\
Logan Currie\
So comparable companies, I can send you a few of the like newer
competitors in this space but the are charging 10% of annual salary. So
like there\'s a company called Jack and Jill that essentially is trying
to replace the recruiter and they\'re like 10% of annual salary. That\'s
the fee that we take. There\'s a few that are taking like 15 to 20%.
Obviously recruiters will take anywhere between you know like 15 to 30%
depending on the role. So like where we are essentially positioning
ourselves in between inbound and like bespoke recruiter.

**\
21:10**\
Ray Batra\
And then to confirm you said you do have is it. You said four employers
right now that have actually agreed to pay. These are sort of across a
variety of industries.

**\
21:18**\
Logan Currie\
Mostly tech. Mostly tech. So this is relevant as well from a faux
perspective. If you guys wanted to like go forward with a pilot, I think
probably the. Well I still have some conversations coming up with some
interesting ed tech people but like the roles that we\'re seeing kind of
more demand for obviously like on the engineering side which is
potentially not as relevant to majority of fohee members but then
product management and so like product centric roles are also where
we\'re seeing a lot of volume and a lot of demand. So those are the two.
I\'d say that we\'re starting with top, first. But like I also again you
guys have relationships, we have relationships. The system functions
regardless of role essentially. So those have just been the first
relationships on the employer side.

**\
22:11**\
Ray Batra\
So and then in terms of volume of jobs. So of those four, like if we
again if were to move forward, if were to sort of just start and
potentially with something small like if you have reason to believe that
with your current employer lineup and sort of who you\'ve seen is
actually willing to pay. If it is going to be probably more product
engineering, like it might make sense to try something where we\'re
limiting expectations or managing expectations properly in among our
community and say if you\'re in product or engineering or something like
this is a place to start. I\'m just thinking a lot and then in terms of
like are each of those companies have each of them posted a role yet or
not yet and is it, do they have any acceptance?

**\
22:56**\
Vrijen Attawar\
We have close to 10 roles that we\'re scanning for on a sort of weekly
basis and we\'ve put forward about like 20 ish candidates. Maybe three
or four have been picked up. Or at least like this is. But.

**\
23:10**\
Logan Currie\
And this is within two weeks. I\'d say like we just signed an additional
12 roles with that one company that just raised a series B on Friday. So
it\'s like the. Again and it\'s. And employers are like, sometimes
they\'re like, no, we want to be stealth. And so like, then we\'re like,
cool, we can be stealth. Like, and so we. We distribute the. The role
without revealing the employer name. So like again, employers are very.
It\'s been a fun adventure to see like all of the ins and.

**\
23:38**\
Vrijen Attawar\
Outs on the Employers are like such a absolutely rattled bunch. They\'re
like, we want you to use our ats, but we also don\'t want too many
people to use it. But we also want to be able to track you, but we
don\'t want to respond to you ever. Like, it\'s a fun group to engage
with.

**\
23:55**\
Jake Weissbourd\
Sounds about right.

**\
23:57**\
Logan Currie\
Is the main value that you\'re communicating to employers is that it
will reduce their time to actually sift through? Not necessarily find.
Because I know a lot of employers are like, we\'re getting so many
applications and obviously right now, especially for engineering roles,
I\'m sure.

**\
24:14**\
Vrijen Attawar\
So yeah, we\'re at the place and you\'re gonna see way more articles and
way more stuff about this. We are very much at the place where like the
entire ability to evaluate people has become obsolete. Right When. When
an AI can reproduce the perfect resume for a job based on just a JD
because all you\'re doing is matching keywords, then that entire like
filtering system is obsolete. We had the. I was on a call with the head
of. Of recruiting or talent for Zapier and she was like, yeah, you know,
we essentially just only use internal tools. I\'ve had six or seven
founders tell me that they only post the job up for legal reasons. They
do not actually look at the inbound. It is. It is largely purely for
political for. For legal reasons that people insisting.

**\
25:06**\
Logan Currie\
So to answer your question, legally, obviously there\'s still very valid
questions around the use of AI in hiring. So where we. Where we are in
your existing process, we would rather be sit outside of your ats, your
inbound. We\'re not agency, but we are introducing you to candidates
that you would not see otherwise, essentially. So we\'re saying like,
listen, this is not anything you need to do. Give us the JD any
additional like info about what you\'re looking for. We\'ll give you
five candidates.

**\
25:37**\
Vrijen Attawar\
Yeah.

**\
25:38**\
Logan Currie\
Five to ten candidates that are phenomenal matches and that you would
not have found otherwise, either because they\'re, like, lost in the
flood of Inbound or your existing referral network that is typically
going to give you the same type of person that you already have.

**\
25:52**\
Ray Batra\
Exactly. Cool. I appreciate it. I know we\'re at time, Jake. Any live
final things, and then I know we can.

**\
